speaker: interviewer
text: The tape number four. Mr. Malkin, I want to hear about the plan itself and how you executed the plan in capturing Eichmann. And before we do, I'd like to just sort of talk a little about Eichmann himself. And this wasn't just a 55, 56-year-old man who you were just capturing, this was-- this was someone who-- who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was responsible for something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was responsible for a lot. And there are a number-- number of stories in your book, some-- most of them horrible. There was one story of Eichmann in Minsk when he went to visit the Einsatz Group. And there was the story with the mother and the-- and the baby that I believe he told you, or you heard him and he talked so dispassionately about. Do you remember that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they're there, but I think we ought to say-- maybe I'll start from another point, if you don't mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is for me, as a young man at that time, when I heard Eichmann-- maybe this is important how I portrayed him there. I can't-- I don't know who he is, you know? It is something extra-- he has to be big. He has to be horrible. He has to be terrible. I don't know what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my imagination worked on me a lot. And I-- I couldn't-- I just prepared myself physically to capture him. And I got this-- in many ways, I exercised-- I maybe caught about 100, 150 people, just like this trying and exercise on the streets. All my relatives or friends thought I'm crazy. Take him. Hold him, because you don't want to-- you know, exercise is one thing. And if they run-- no, is it something else? So I made all these kinds of exercises.

speaker: interviewee
text: And still, I want to say the truth is that I still didn't know it was Eichmann. Even I heard all these stories, and these stories were that we have-- have had a fight, and we have collected many things that he's done. But it was not important for me to hear the stories exactly in Minsk and all this. But all-- but already-- but later, when I talked to him and I had the chance-- that was, I say, my-- I want to understand more.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the beginning, what was important is, was important about him, what is  the size? How much he weighs. How does he go? What does he dress? Does he have a wallet with him? Is he coming alone? Where is the way? All this stuff was important. You have to remember, as an operator, I have to think about these cold things. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: These things was more important than what he has done. Because what he has done, we'll hear later. That was very important to make the exercise with the capture, and also very important how he behaves, and who's protecting him, and what is he. And sometimes, you'll see things, and you don't know many things, because he is your opponent. You never know if he dated somebody, somebody will come out of the house, the police is coming. You never know. And you have to take all these precautions. So what I've studied, from the moment I got this message, is only one thing-- exercises, and also to try that the capture will be excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Because you have to remember, to capture a man is not a big thing today, capturing, all this. For us, it's very important to capture-- nobody will see it-- and to bring to Israel, and to leave the country, if no signs of evidence of any aggressive thing had happened. It's not easy, this, because somebody's missing, right? Somebody's missing, right? What is the police is going to do? What are his sons are going to do, or his relatives? You know, the South America was not, at that time-- and I don't know how they are now-- was not a place that you-- for money, you can get information, you get-- you get the police-- [COUGHS] Many things that we have to know. So there was-- the-- the-- the-- the most important was the gathering of the information. What he was and how I talked to him, that, we'll see later. So that-- I want to go, in purpose, to go into how I acted and what was the process of-- of doing the thing. So excuse me, if I don't answer exactly. Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- that was the first time. But still, in-- in a good operation is, whatever you get information [INAUDIBLE], you'll receive [? COEIAS, ?] [? C-O-E-I-A-S. ?] You read, it's still not enough. In the end, you have to see the place of act. That's the most important. For me, was the important thing is was the place where I'm going to catch him. So being before, was Hans there. And we're looking after him. One was looking, and he didn't believe that that's the guy, because he lived in a poor place. And he didn't believe it could be that he's poor, because he could be the richest man in the world. And he saw him, like loved his family. And his name was Ricardo Klement. So we thought, maybe, it's really the family. It could be that the son is called Eichmann. But still, that-- I think her name was Vera-- that she remarried somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we couldn't-- I would say, we couldn't, in the beginning, know if it's him or not. Why? Because you don't have to forget that Eichmann, when he was dressed, you know, in the uniform, was somebody, and 15 years older. And he took off the uniform and aged more, 15 years old-- 15 years. So it was not the same man. And it was difficult to understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the first agent that we sent, Major [INAUDIBLE], is not the man. And only after one and a half years later, we came back. So it took about two and a half years after the information that Fritz Bauer, [INAUDIBLE] security, insisted that we didn't do a good work. And you know, it contradicts sometimes that they say about-- about [? Riesentag, ?] that he was involved in  all this. I don't know about his involvement. It's not because I like [? Riesentag, ?] and I hate-- I don't mind who, but it really was the prosecutor of [? Hassen, ?] [? Franz Joseph-- ?] that-- Fritz Bauer-- sorry-- [? Franz Joseph-- ?] Fritz Bauer, that was the man, the man who did it. And he has to get the credit. And he-- so he insisted. And we-- this time, we came a full team, a full team to capture him and to know. So we sent first Hans. He was there photographing his chin, his nose. It didn't contradict his-- his-- his face that we got in the file from the Americans. It didn't contradict. The nose could be the nose, could be the chin. Could be, but not for sure. Nothing was sure. And we knew that only-- and we had also evidence. We knew that there is a number of the SS under his arm. We knew that he has broken his ribs in an accident. We knew his size of the-- of the head. We knew the way how-- how the teeth, the number of his shoes, and all these kind of-- there was about 12 question that it has to be the same. And we knew the number of his SS number, not only here, but [NON-ENGLISH]. It was here also. So-- and then, after following him, a few weeks before, and then I came there, and I saw him how he walked and what car he goes. I knew his children. I knew the small boy. I knew the place. It was raining. It was winter at the time, very-- very cold. And it could be very cold days in the-- in Buenos Aires.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was laying there in the water, not once, with my binoculars and looking into there. I saw him, how he lives, how the woman, and how he likes the small boy. And I said, maybe we are mistaken. How could it be that Eichmann loves so the small child and loves his family is the man who killed so many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: You never know. I said, maybe we're mistaken. The name is Ricardo Klement. Everybody knows him as Ricardo Klement. Well, the question-- the question mark. And we didn't know. But then, it came to all the-- if we are going after him, if we're following him anymore, it will be a catastrophe, because something will happen, that he will feel. Because it was a place that you don't have a reason to stay there in San Fernando in the Garibaldi Street, and a place that every-- everybody knows everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, the question is, what are you doing? You touring there? OK, what are you doing? So that's why we have to be so far, laying in the dark, and looking in there. And see the car, and all this. Until, I made the plan in my head where I'm going to catch him and how I'm going to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's very important two things has to be-- that the most important thing in the kidnap, in this case, was that you wouldn't be recognized. It has to be as fast as possible. That's one thing. And has to be the decision that nobody sees you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, even you're going to capture, you have, always, to have to have the alternative not to do it. Understand? Sometimes, in the life of an agent, it's the most important thing not to do it, than more than to do. When you do it, and you can't, how you say, stop it, and somebody knows you, you finish your job, and you finish also your career. So you have to do it in a way that was not the question of career. If we-- if we feel something, or something will happen, no way you're going to capture ever again Eichmann. You will never see him again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you have to remember, when I-- the night when I was working, we stood there with two cars. One car was far, and one car near in. And he went down from the path and started walking. And I was on the other side. I knew only one sentence. Uno momentito, senor. One moment, sir. That I can stop and talk to him like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what I have to see in the beginning is that nobody in the surrounding could see this aggressive act of capture him. His children wouldn't come out from the home, and no police will be there. And my head was full with Tel Aviv, and my mother, and everything, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the question is of concentration was very important, and very important to see that nobody sees. And you have to decide that this is-- this moment is the act you're going to do it. And we have to do it in nine seconds. Is an act that, even you see something, you couldn't distinguish at night-- you know, in the evening, at about 8 o'clock, 8:20, that some act-- even you see something, you couldn't believe it is an act of kidnapping. Not every day, you see that. If it's prolong itself, so it's a bad thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second thing has some-- so very quickly, and if somebody isn't surrounding. And that was the decision. I said, that's the moment. When I said, uno momentito, he looked at me. He hold his hand in the pocket. Somebody warned, maybe as a pistol. There's no retreat, you know? I captured him. I-- and I got-- and I saw he's going back. I jumped on him. He fall. We fall in the ditch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they say that he shouted. I still don't remember the shouting. They say the shouting. But let's say it could be, because I didn't want to hold him too strong, because my boss, Israel [INAUDIBLE] said, if you strangle him, I strangle you. [LAUGHS] Because he has to-- what-- well, all-- the whole thing was to bring him-- not revenge, is to bring him to Israel [COUGHS] for a trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this plan of him walking down the street-- and as I recall, your-- your colleague was-- had the hood up of a car and was making like there was something wrong with it, as he would pass by. And then, you'd pass him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a car, you know? The hood was up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was not the original plan. That was-- the original plan was given to you, and you reject it, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was another plan, you know? That-- there was a plan that it would be two cars, far away on a street. I would stand there. I would capture him, and then they would come. And I said, no. No way. I can't accept it, because you have-- in this kind of thing, coordination is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did I do it myself? Because I could get another one to capture him. At night, you coordinate. I hold him from here, you hold him from here, you'll tear him to pieces. And I'm not sure that we coordinate right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, coordination, if you set, one man can coordinate with his hands and feet. And he knows exactly what he's doing. Also, when you have to start-- when I capture him, and he will see me, these two cars. Was no-- it was a very-- a risky thing, because cars were coming. The reflection at night-- I will say the-- the lights can blind you for more than one-- a long-- a long time. And I would stand there and hold him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody said his hands, head, OK. You know what? So you-- you will say-- I said, how to be a hero, I know myself. Let's finish it. It will be the-- the exact-- what you have to do is say-- we say 9 seconds, 20 seconds? Has to be, when I capture him, the car has to be nearer. But the problem was always if he will walk through, he will see a car. What will happen? I said nothing will happen. That was the problem. Israel said-- Israel-- they have Israel, and we gave him the plan. [COUGHS] Maybe we retreat. I said-- and maybe we will go from other side. I said, no, there's a lot of mud there. Was all the time raining. I haven't seen an officer, a German officer, that he will go into the mud with shiny shoes. No way, he'll will do it. Second, [COUGHS] you have, in yourself, you have self-respect. You don't retreat when you are very near to the home. Where are you going to retreat, if you are frightened? You just continue, you act, and you go to home and say, you know what? I am frightened, but still go to home.

speaker: interviewee
text: It happens, you know, that the car in the rain happens something [INAUDIBLE]. So the decision will be I will continue. Even wouldn't continue, also I would have captured him, if the rain, the rain, all this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the decision was, yes, that the car will be nearer. And somebody will be-- and the hood up. If they-- if something happened to me, or something, I need help, he will come to help me. So I did it exactly. I jumped on him, fell into the ditch. I took him on me. I hold him strong, a little bit, and brought him up. Somebody came. He put them in the car. And we were on  our way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened, really, that one car didn't arrive, because it happened to me with the lights. He couldn't see even that-- that the car disappeared. It's only later that the car came, because of this light. So this light. You see, it's like a small team. So somebody says, like this. Somebody said, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of your paintings that we're going to be taking a look at, one of the paintings is a man with a bicycle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's the man. There was a boy always coming through. You know, he brings tea, coffee, and all this. And the-- on-- on-- on exactly the time when he arrives, it could be that he will appear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one day, and a few minutes before, he said, can I help you? We see the hood up. No, no, no. No, no. We didn't understand exactly what he says. So we get rid of him, thanks God. And he went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, it's always the big cars going on there, on the 202. That's a big road. It's all the time-- you never know what car will come in through Garibaldi Street, or walking. When he came down, he came with a woman. They separated. He started to walk. I jumped on him, got him. We put him in the car. And that was the-- I'll say the break point-- I don't know how you call it-- in me. When I hold his hand, and somebody said, you could say a word, it'd be dead, something like this. The Hans said it. It was not needed, because I hold him in his mouth. And he nodded. He said, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I wanted to tell, also, before-- You made a decision--

words.text: I wanted to tell, also, before--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the capture, I said to myself, I am going to capture him. I can't do it with my hands. I have to do something. I have to buy gloves, or something. I didn't say anything. There are many times I didn't say everything to-- to my superiors.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just went to the town, purchased gloves. They had been a little bit short-- short. I said, OK, I buy them. And I have to have something that I couldn't touch his mouth. I don't like to touch any mouth, you know? But this mouth that gave so many orders of killing, I can't do it. I brought the gloves, you know? And we'll see, maybe, the sculpture of it. And as I said, they got the gloves now, in the Museum of Human Heritage. They have it now. And whenever it will appear-- when the-- when the museum will appear, [CHUCKLES] this-- these gloves will appear. And I caught him with the gloves. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] So now, what happened to me is, when I hold him, and I said, how is it-- and that was really it happened to me-- how is it that this guy, like me and you, a man like me and you, did all these atrocities, killing of people, children, women? How could it happen that he is the man? I couldn't believe it. And I said, and if he is the guy, what makes a man to be-- to become Eichmann? I have to know it. And suddenly, it came to me like, you know, a feeling that I have to ask him about it. I didn't know exactly how it would be, but this was the turning point in me. I said-- the question was, why-- how did you do it? Who is he? How did it become that he became Eichmann, such a man that he ordered millions of people, 1 million of children, to go to their death in the concentration camps? I had to understand. And at the time, you were under orders not to speak with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Normally, nobody said to you not to speak, you know? It's-- it's-- it's for granted that you don't speak. Yeah. You are a man of a operation. Normally, you don't have to-- I never had a problem to speak, because I never met people, I met documents. I don't have a problem to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, the orders are, you don't speak with a spy, or this kind of thing, not a criminal. He was something else. And that's what happened to me. I asked myself, why shouldn't I speak with him? I-- it was-- nobody told me not to speak. It's normally that, traditionally, you don't speak. Somebody else will speak with him. You captured him. Good bye. Thank you. You hold him. OK. Speaking? An interrogator will do it. But that's a problem. I asked myself, why shouldn't I talk to him? He's a criminal. He killed my-- he gave orders to kill my sister, children, and my relatives, and all this. What's the big thing if I know how he did it? What is it? It's against the law? Or it's against tradition? Or it's against something, that you can't do it? OK. It's only, maybe, it will-- wouldn't-- it was not so good for the interrogation. But who cares, I said to myself? [INAUDIBLE] didn't like this man what the interrogation has. But you know, he was really a German traditional and all this. In a way, it went-- it brought me to this, that he is a-- how do you say? He reminded me a little bit of this German tradition. I didn't like German tradition. I speak, and I question, and all this. OK. So I-- after we came into the room, and the question was, well, is he Eichmann, or not? So we asked him. The first question was, the interrogator, what's your name? So in German, he said, I'm Ricardo Klement. Again, what's your name? Ricardo Klement. We felt, oy vey, you know? Didn't feel so good, even with it was not true, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we undressed him. And we knew that he has a-- the number under his arm. It was erased. Was a mark there, erased. We saw the-- I say, the accident. We saw exactly where the accident was right. The number of the head was right. This was right. Then we asked him about the number of his-- number of his-- Gestapo number. And he said-- we gave him some numbers, and he helped it, you know, somehow. No, no, this is number this and this. In the end of the 20 minutes, he said, ich bin Adolf Eichmann, I am Adolf Eichmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we dressed him. We put him on a-- everything was ready, the room. Were not too many things there. One bedroom, that he was chained-- chained to his leg. Only we didn't need so much the chain, but OK. All the other operators would be in the other side of this villa. And [INAUDIBLE] was, I put him in the bed, and he was laying there. [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a high chair there to watch him. And Naomi was the veranda there. The-- a friend of mine was there. The uzi was sitting there. The other people with the woman was the kitchen nearby-- had the kitchen and the room that I slept nearby, sometimes. And the woman that I slept with her together. Was only one bed. [LAUGHS] We slept there, because I have to be near to him. But most of the time-- most of the time, I was with him. I had this small book, the red, small book that I was reading, the diary of South America, how you come to South America-- this small, red book. And I, most of the time, was painting, you know, and the diary, because I had disguise, and all this makeup, this kind of [INAUDIBLE] disguising him, was also painting in this book. And it came out that, later, I painted about 60 small paintings during my time. I didn't know even--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you actually painted right onto the pages of the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the pages of the book. On the pages of the book, both sides. I didn't know I never will use it, or not. But I didn't have what to do, so I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, one day, after about one day or two, I didn't think he spoke. And later, suddenly, I had him asking me, are you the man who captured-- captured me? I said, how do you know? I know, because I remember still the words uno momentito, senor. And-- and that gave me the opening to ask him questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Holocaust, you know, is a very difficult thing, as I said before. You can use the Holocaust-- it's a [? rule ?] for many, many stories. And it's not-- I don't justify the name, Holocaust, because there have been many brave stories, children's stories, women. And the world, as I said before, is frightening and the-- if you don't come to the read the stories. Anne Frank, you'll hear, is not a Holocaust story. It's a Holocaust-- it is a story for a young woman in a situation that gives you some very brave and very wonderful story. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so what I want to say is that, to talk to him, is only because he questioned me and all this. Now, what I want to say is-- something else is-- [COUGHS] when you-- you say millions of people, I said I've never seen 6 million people. And I don't-- can't understand it. I don't think anybody can see 6 million laying on-- on-- on one place, and you can see it, describe it. Nobody can describe it. Many times, 6 million, and all this kind of thing. I said, no. What I want to do is I want to ask him very simple questions that bother me the most, is about the son. Because I've seen him many times playing with his son and all this. It remind me of the son of my sister. He was also blonde. He had blonde, blue eyes, more blonde than his son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I want to ask him about this son. And the first question was, could you tell me, why is your son alive? Six years old, the blonde, blue eyes. He said, did you kill him? I said, no. Let me finish the question. I said, and the son of my sister is dead. Why is it? And after all, he understood already, he had-- and he said, wasn't he Jewish? I said, yes. What can I do? That was my order. And I said, and you didn't come to an order to capture me? You see, we are all soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, look. I came to capture you, to capture a criminal. I never came to kill. And there were never-- I didn't do anything to your son. We didn't do anything to your family. Even to you, we didn't do. We captured you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ask me if I killed we-- you-- about your son. We could kill you. We could hurt your son easily. Through the window, we could many times kill you. We didn't-- we came to bring you to the court of Jerusalem. That is our purpose. Yeah, Jerusalem, he said, Jerusalem? Oh, my. He was very frightened. I said, we came to bring you. You see? And you, what you have done, you compare me to you, that we are all soldiers? What chance did you give to my sister or to other women? To the children? You brought them, till the end, to these gas chambers. And you told the children that they only go to the-- to the showers. Even you didn't give them a chance to say goodbye. Sometimes, you took them with them. You separated them and didn't give them a chance to say goodbye. And you compare me to you? That I came as a soldier to bring you?
